text: An epigastric Nathanson retractor is used to lift the left lobe of the liver, providing clear exposure to the gastroesophageal junction and the angle of His.
The surgeon identifies a small type II paraesophageal hernia and visualizes a replaced left hepatic artery during the procedure.
The video shows a slipped Nissen fundoplication, with the wrap visibly displaced distally over the stomach and the sutures detached from the esophagus.
The hernia sac is observed positioned above the anterior aspect of the gastric wrap.
The surgeon uses a forced bipolar device and monopolar curved scissors to carefully dissect and remove adhesions from the wrap after docking the robotic system.
The surgeon dissects and divides the three previously placed fundoplication sutures, which are visible around the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon dissects the hernia sac free from the diaphragm and anterior wrap, then divides the sac.
The surgeon achieves circumferential dissection around the esophagus, revealing the structure completely surrounded by dissected tissue.
A fourteen-inch Penrose drain is placed around the gastroesophageal (GE) junction to provide gentle traction.
The surgeon continues the dissection to take down the previous fundoplication wrap, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the wrap from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon observes that the anterior hiatus has dilated after achieving good mobilization, while the posterior sutures remain intact.
The surgeon continues to circumferentially mobilize the esophagus, ensuring it is free from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon achieves adequate mobilization of the esophagus, revealing five to six centimeters of intra-abdominal length.
The surgeon closes the posterior crura using a 2-0 Ethibond suture, cut to a length of twenty-five centimeters, securing the anatomical structure.
The surgeon ties four knots for each stitch, securing the sutures in place.
The surgeon places an anterior suture to prevent kinking of the esophagus, ensuring proper alignment and tension.
The surgeon mobilizes the gastric fundus and passes it posteriorly behind the esophagus, demonstrating the ease of maneuvering these anatomical structures.
A 58 French bougie is inserted into the esophagus, and a 270-degree Toupet fundoplication is performed using three sutures to wrap the fundus around the esophagus.
The surgeon secures the wrap leaflets to the posterior crura using a single stitch, completing the fixation of the fundoplication wrap.
The surgeon places a superior suture that secures the esophageal muscle, fundus, and superior crura together.
The surgeon places the second inferior suture, securing the esophageal muscle to the fundus.
The surgeon carefully identifies and protects both the anterior and vagus nerves during the procedure, ensuring these structures are not damaged.
The surgeon carefully positions the fundoplication above the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring proper placement of the wrap.
The surgeon carefully removes the bougie from the esophagus, ensuring complete hemostasis in the left upper quadrant, with no visible bleeding.
An upper endoscopy is performed intraoperatively, visually confirming that the fundoplication wrap is positioned above the z-line.
The endoscopic retroflexion view reveals a 270-degree wrap around the esophagus, confirming the placement of the fundoplication.
The surgeon retrieves the needles and Penrose drain, removes all instruments, and undocks the robotic system from the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the surgical site, confirming the absence of visible esophageal or gastric injuries.
